title: Technology-Challenged Customer Needing Basic Training
primary_situation: Customer lacks confidence using the app and needs help with basic features in order to get value from the platform.
customer_goal: Learn core features quickly enough to use the platform in the field without frustration.
opening_line: I am not very good with this app stuff and I need help figuring out the basics.
known_facts: Customer admits low proficiency with the app.
They may need immediate help with mobile features or downloading storm maps.
They need patient, accessible support.
hidden_facts: They may be worried about looking incompetent.
They are more likely to stay if support feels nonjudgmental.
They may benefit more from live guidance than written instructions.
likely_objections: I do not know how to use this on my phone.
This feels too complicated for me.
I need someone to walk me through it.
red_lines: Using jargon without explanation
Implying the customer should already know this
Rushing through support

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer is receptive and willing to follow instructions.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is frustrated and needs repeated reassurance.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is embarrassed, defensive, and considering quitting because the platform feels too difficult.

scoring_signals: Rep normalizes the learning curve
Rep explains features in simple language
Rep offers immediate and scheduled support options
Rep confirms understanding before moving on
Rep closes with a clear training next step

title: Confused About Coverage Area and Pricing
primary_situation: Customer is unclear about what territories are included in their current plan and what it would cost to expand coverage.
customer_goal: Understand exactly what is covered now and what options exist to add more areas.
opening_line: I need to understand exactly what areas I am paying for and what it would cost to add more.
known_facts: Customer is unsure which states or cities are included.
They may believe they have coverage they do not actually have.
They are evaluating renewal or expansion decisions.
hidden_facts: They may feel prior plan details were not clear.
They may be comparing current usage to future territory needs.
They are more likely to renew if the explanation is specific and transparent.
likely_objections: I thought this area was already included.
Why would I need to pay extra for that territory?
I do not want surprises after renewing.
red_lines: Speaking vaguely about coverage boundaries
Avoiding exact pricing details
Assuming the customer understands plan terminology

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer simply needs a straightforward clarification.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer asks detailed questions and challenges prior assumptions.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer feels misled and questions whether renewal is worth it.

scoring_signals: Rep clearly explains current coverage
Rep identifies the exact gap between expectation and plan
Rep outlines pricing and territory options accurately
Rep avoids ambiguous language
Rep confirms the customer understands next steps

title: Budget-Constrained Customer Seeking Flexibility
primary_situation: Customer values HailTrace but cannot afford current renewal terms right now.
customer_goal: Reduce immediate financial burden without fully losing access.
opening_line: I like the platform, but I just cannot make this renewal work at that price right now.
known_facts: Customer has used the service and sees value.
Cash flow is tight this month.
They want flexibility, not necessarily cancellation.
hidden_facts: They may accept split billing if explained clearly.
They have not fully reviewed lower-tier options.
They are more likely to stay if the rep shows understanding before selling.
likely_objections: We just do not have room in the budget.
I cannot commit to the full annual amount today.
I need something more flexible.
allowed_resolution_paths: delayed restart date if policy allows
red_lines: Pushy upsell before acknowledging budget reality
Vague promises without specifics
Dismissing their loyalty

difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is cautious and asks detailed payment questions.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is skeptical because they feel prior promises were unclear.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges value and budget pressure
Rep diagnoses whether issue is timing, amount, or plan fit
Rep presents approved flexibility options clearly
Rep does not invent discounts or policies
Rep closes on a concrete next step

title: Leadership Change or New Decision-Maker
primary_situation: A new stakeholder is now responsible for the account and is unfamiliar with the platform, its features, and prior purchasing rationale.
customer_goal: Assess whether the current subscription still makes sense under new leadership.
opening_line: I just stepped into this role and I need to understand why we have this and whether we still need it.
known_facts: A new decision-maker has joined mid-contract.
Previous leadership may have purchased features that were underused.
The customer needs orientation before making renewal decisions.
hidden_facts: They may be looking for cost reductions to establish control.
They may distrust inherited vendor relationships.
They are more likely to stay if value is tied to business outcomes instead of feature lists.
likely_objections: I do not know how much we are actually using this.
Why are we paying for these features?
I need to reevaluate all our vendors.
red_lines: Relying on old assumptions about prior leadership
Defensiveness when asked to justify value
Overloading the customer with feature details without context

difficulty_modifiers.easy: New stakeholder is open and simply needs context.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Stakeholder is comparing vendors and scrutinizing spend.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Stakeholder believes the platform was a poor purchase from the start.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges the leadership transition
Rep explains value in business terms
Rep identifies adoption gaps without blame
Rep proposes a structured review or onboarding plan
Rep creates a credible next step for stakeholder alignment

title: Customer Working Outside Coverage Area
primary_situation: Customer is actively working jobs in a location outside their coverage area and cannot access needed storm data.
customer_goal: Get access or clarity fast enough to support active field work.
opening_line: We are out working this area right now and I cannot access the storm data I need.
known_facts: Customer is operating outside their existing territory.
They may not have realized territorial limitations before traveling.
They need practical options quickly.
hidden_facts: They may blame the platform if expectations were unclear.
They may be willing to pay for expanded coverage if the process is fast.
They may be under pressure from jobs already in progress.
likely_objections: I thought we would be able to use this here.
Why am I locked out when I need it most?
We are already on the ground in this market.
allowed_resolution_paths: temporary territory expansion discussion if allowed
red_lines: Acting casual about the urgency
Blaming the customer for not knowing coverage limits
Giving uncertain answers about access

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer just needs a quick explanation and option set.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is frustrated but willing to discuss expansion.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer feels misled and says the platform failed during a critical moment.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges operational urgency
Rep clearly explains territory limitations
Rep presents approved access options promptly
Rep does not overpromise immediate access if policy does not allow it
Rep ends with a concrete path forward

title: Account Access Issues Due to Email Problems
primary_situation: Customer cannot receive reports or access the account because their primary email was hacked, changed, or is otherwise no longer usable.
customer_goal: Restore secure access and make sure the right people receive account communications.
opening_line: Our email situation changed and now we are not getting the reports or account notifications.
known_facts: Primary email access is compromised or outdated.
Reports and renewal notifications may not be reaching the right person.
Multiple users or ownership transfer may be needed.
hidden_facts: Customer may worry about account security exposure.
They may be unsure who currently controls the account.
They are more likely to trust support that follows a clear verification process.
likely_objections: We do not have access to that email anymore.
Important reports are going to the wrong place.
We need this fixed right away.
red_lines: Skipping security verification
Being vague about what can and cannot be changed
Minimizing the seriousness of the email issue

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer has authority and needed verification details ready.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer needs guidance through admin changes and user setup.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Ownership is unclear and the customer is upset about missed communications.

scoring_signals: Rep treats the issue as security-sensitive
Rep explains verification and change steps clearly
Rep addresses both access and reporting impacts
Rep offers appropriate escalation when needed
Rep closes with a secure next step

title: Confusion Between Pause and Cancellation
primary_situation: Customer believed the account was cancelled, but it was only paused and later resumed, leading to unexpected charges.
customer_goal: Understand what happened and resolve billing frustration fairly.
opening_line: I thought this account was cancelled, so why did I get charged again?
known_facts: Customer believed cancellation had been completed.
Subscription resumed after a pause.
There may have been a prior communication or follow-up gap.
hidden_facts: Customer may feel trust was broken by the company.
They may reference something a prior rep said.
They are more likely to calm down if the rep first acknowledges the confusion and impact.
likely_objections: I was told this would be cancelled.
Nobody followed up with me.
I should not have been charged.
red_lines: Arguing with the customer before reviewing context
Using technical wording to avoid accountability
Dismissing prior agent communication concerns

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer wants an explanation and a fair correction path.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is frustrated and demands specific billing answers.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is angry, distrustful, and threatens dispute or churn.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges the confusion and unexpected charge
Rep clearly explains the difference between pause and cancellation
Rep reviews facts before making conclusions
Rep follows policy and escalates billing concerns appropriately
Rep leaves the customer with a concrete resolution path

title: Unpaid Invoice Blocking Access Before Major Weather Event
primary_situation: Customer is locked out because of an unpaid invoice right before a major weather event when access is business-critical.
customer_goal: Restore access quickly or understand the fastest path to do so.
opening_line: There is weather moving in and we need access now, but the account is blocked over an invoice.
known_facts: Access is blocked due to unpaid billing.
Timing is critical because of an active weather period.
The decision-maker may be unavailable or hesitant.
hidden_facts: Customer may be trying to buy time while internal approval catches up.
They may question feature usage before approving payment.
They are highly sensitive to slow or procedural responses.
likely_objections: We cannot wait until next week for this.
The person who approves payment is unavailable.
We need the service before this weather weekend.
allowed_resolution_paths: billing options discussion if policy allows
red_lines: Ignoring the urgency of the weather event
Making promises about reactivation without approval
Lecturing the customer on billing discipline

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer can resolve payment quickly once guided.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer needs help navigating internal approval pressure.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is locked out, upset, and feels the timing makes the situation unacceptable.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges the timing sensitivity
Rep explains the billing block clearly
Rep presents the fastest approved path to resolution
Rep escalates appropriately without inventing exceptions
Rep leaves the customer with an immediate next action

title: Perceived Poor Value Due to Limited Storm Activity
primary_situation: Customer feels they paid for a year of service but saw little return because storm activity did not produce usable leads in their area.
customer_goal: Decide whether renewal is worth it given inconsistent storm opportunities.
opening_line: We paid for the year, but the storms just were not there for us, so it feels like a waste.
known_facts: Customer had limited useful storm opportunities during the term.
Storms may have missed their service radius.
They are questioning renewal due to inconsistent local activity.
hidden_facts: They may understand this is partly weather variability, but still want value justification.
They may need help reframing value beyond a single season.
They are less likely to renew if the rep argues with their lived experience.
likely_objections: We did not get enough leads from this.
It is hard to justify paying again.
Storm activity in our area is too inconsistent.
red_lines: Telling the customer their perception is wrong
Overstating expected storm volume
Ignoring local market variability

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer is open to a broader value discussion.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer presses hard on ROI and local results.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer sees the entire subscription as wasted spend.

scoring_signals: Rep validates the customer's disappointment
Rep separates platform value from uncontrollable weather variability carefully
Rep explores plan fit and territory strategy
Rep avoids guaranteeing future storm outcomes
Rep proposes a realistic next step around renewal or fit

title: Underutilizing Features Due to Lack of Training
primary_situation: Customer bought the service but never fully onboarded, so they are missing valuable features and integrations.
customer_goal: Understand what they are missing and how to start getting full value from the platform.
opening_line: I know we probably are not using this fully, but I do not really know what we are missing.
known_facts: Customer never completed onboarding or training.
They may not know about included features or CRM integrations.
They are underutilizing the service.
hidden_facts: They may feel some guilt about not adopting it properly.
They may renew if shown concrete unrealized value.
They need practical enablement, not just a feature dump.
likely_objections: We never really got this set up properly.
I did not know that feature was included.
We have not had time to learn all of this.
red_lines: Blaming the customer for low adoption
Dumping too many features at once
Ignoring the need for structured onboarding

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer is ready to engage in training.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is busy and needs a concise value-focused path.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer doubts it is worth investing time into onboarding now.

scoring_signals: Rep identifies missed onboarding as the core issue
Rep connects underused features to real customer outcomes
Rep offers specific training or integration help
Rep avoids blame and focuses on enablement
Rep sets a concrete onboarding next step

title: Duplicate Services Creating Cost Concerns
primary_situation: Customer is paying for another service with overlapping functionality and is questioning whether HailTrace is redundant.
customer_goal: Determine what unique value HailTrace provides versus the other platform.
opening_line: We already pay for another service that gives us similar data, so I need to know why we should keep both.
known_facts: Customer already uses another service.
They see possible overlap in functionality.
They are evaluating whether both subscriptions are necessary.
hidden_facts: They may already be leaning toward cutting one platform.
They may be testing whether the rep can articulate differentiated value.
They will respond better to honest comparison than defensive selling.
likely_objections: This seems duplicative.
Why should we keep paying for both?
I am not sure the extra cost is justified.
red_lines: Claiming superiority without specifics
Attacking the competitor in vague terms
Ignoring overlap concerns

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer wants a fair comparison and is open-minded.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer cites overlap and asks detailed value questions.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer already believes HailTrace is the one to cut.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges overlap without defensiveness
Rep clearly differentiates HailTrace's value
Rep ties differentiation to the customer's workflow
Rep avoids unsupported competitor claims
Rep advances the conversation toward a decision-worthy next step

title: Notification Overload from Wrong Settings
primary_situation: Customer is receiving too many irrelevant alerts because notification settings are misconfigured.
customer_goal: Reduce noise and make alerts relevant to their actual coverage area and workflow.
opening_line: I am getting way too many alerts from areas I do not even work, and it is becoming useless.
known_facts: Customer is receiving excessive notifications.
Alerts may be enabled for too broad a region, including nationwide notifications.
They want settings corrected, not a sales conversation.
hidden_facts: They may be close to turning notifications off entirely.
They may assume the system is poorly designed if support is not precise.
They are more likely to be satisfied if the rep resolves the issue simply and fast.
likely_objections: These alerts are not relevant to me.
I am getting storms outside my area.
There are too many notifications to keep up with.
red_lines: Treating the problem as trivial
Explaining settings in an overly technical way
Trying to upsell before fixing the alert issue

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer just needs a settings correction.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer is frustrated and needs guided configuration help.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer no longer trusts the alerts and is considering disengaging.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges the noise problem clearly
Rep identifies likely settings causing irrelevant alerts
Rep provides a precise correction path
Rep keeps the explanation simple
Rep confirms the customer's desired notification scope

title: Expecting Refund After Limited Usage
primary_situation: Customer wants a refund or service extension because weather, seasonality, or technical issues limited their ability to use the platform.
customer_goal: Recover value they feel they lost from unused service time.
opening_line: We could not really use the service the way we expected, so I think we should get a refund or extension.
known_facts: Customer had limited usage due to seasonal slowdown or system issues.
They feel they did not receive full value.
They may be asking for refund, credit, or extension.
hidden_facts: They may be testing whether support is empowered to make concessions.
They may accept alternatives if the reasoning is clear and respectful.
They are sensitive to language that sounds dismissive of lost business time.
likely_objections: We barely used it during that period.
Weather kept us from working.
I do not think we should pay full price for that.
allowed_resolution_paths: renewal or extension alternatives if allowed
red_lines: Promising refunds without policy basis
Blaming the customer for seasonal constraints
Shutting down the conversation without empathy

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer mainly wants to feel heard and understand policy.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer pushes for a credit or exception.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is upset, cites multiple usage barriers, and expects compensation.

scoring_signals: Rep acknowledges the customer's perceived lost value
Rep explains applicable policy clearly
Rep reviews relevant usage or issue context before concluding
Rep escalates appropriately when needed
Rep avoids unauthorized promises while preserving trust

title: Competitive Threat from Lower-Priced Alternative
primary_situation: Customer is actively evaluating or has moved to a lower-priced competitor and is questioning whether HailTrace is worth the premium.
customer_goal: Compare price and value to decide whether to stay, return, or switch fully.
opening_line: Another provider is offering similar coverage for less, so I need to know why I should stay with HailTrace.
known_facts: Customer is evaluating or already using a lower-priced competitor.
They may cite specific pricing and features.
They are looking for value differentiation and possibly retention concessions.
hidden_facts: They may be using competitor pricing as leverage.
They may value specific premium features if clearly articulated.
They are unlikely to respond to generic claims of being better.
likely_objections: They are cheaper than you.
I can get similar data elsewhere.
Why should I pay more for this?
red_lines: Unsupported competitor bashing
Making up price-matching authority
Avoiding the direct pricing challenge

difficulty_modifiers.easy: Customer is exploring and open to comparison.
difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer has a concrete competitor quote and expects a response.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer has already switched and sees little reason to come back.

scoring_signals: Rep addresses the pricing issue directly
Rep differentiates value with specifics
Rep does not invent competitor facts or discounts
Rep aligns the conversation to the customer's business use case
Rep closes on a concrete retention or follow-up path

title: Payment Method Issues Causing Service Interruption
primary_situation: Customer's payment method failed because of expiration, fraud replacement, or credit limit, causing suspension at a critical time.
customer_goal: Update payment quickly and restore service with minimal disruption.
opening_line: Our card changed and now the account is suspended, and we need this turned back on as soon as possible.
known_facts: Card on file expired, was replaced, or failed.
Service interruption is tied to payment failure.
Timing may be especially sensitive because the customer is traveling or working active storms.
hidden_facts: Customer may view this as an administrative issue that should be easy to fix.
They may need confidence that reactivation will be handled quickly once payment is resolved.
They are less interested in explanations than in fast resolution.
likely_objections: The card issue was unintentional.
We need access restored right away.
This is bad timing for a payment problem.
red_lines: Being slow or vague about next steps
Making reactivation promises without confirming process
Treating it like a low-priority billing question

difficulty_modifiers.medium: Customer needs help finding the right payer or method.
difficulty_modifiers.hard: Customer is in a critical business moment and is highly frustrated by the interruption.

scoring_signals: Rep identifies payment failure as the root cause quickly
Rep explains how to update billing clearly
Rep gives an accurate reactivation path
Rep handles the issue with urgency
Rep closes with a specific immediate next action
